name: Thule Society Full
description: The Thule Society (Thule-Gesellschaft) was a German occultist and völkisch group founded in Munich in 1918. Named after a mythical northern land in Greco-Roman geography, it is primarily known as the organization that sponsored the Deutsche Arbeiterpartei (German Workers' Party), which later became the Nazi Party. The Society promoted esoteric ideologies, including racial theories, anti-Semitism, and the belief in a coming Aryan messiah. Proponents claim the Thule Society sought to revive ancient Aryan wisdom and harness occult powers for political and nationalistic purposes. They cite the society's interest in Germanic mythology, runes, and Atlantis as evidence of their esoteric pursuits and alleged influence on Nazi ideology. Mainstream historians and scholars generally view the Thule Society as a minor fringe group whose members, though influential in early Nazi circles, exaggerated their own importance and whose occult beliefs were more of a superficial flavoring than a driving force behind Nazi policies. Criticisms focus on the lack of concrete evidence linking the society directly to Nazi atrocities and the tendency to overstate the influence of occultism on the Nazi movement, attributing it more to social, economic, and political factors prevalent in post-World War I Germany. The Thule Society dissolved in 1925, but its legacy continues to fuel conspiracy theories connecting occultism, Nazism, and the search for lost Aryan origins.
subtitle: Occult origins of Nazism?

claims.claim: The Thule Society was a key influence on Nazi ideology.
claims.evidence: Several early Nazi leaders were members of the Thule Society, including Rudolf Hess and Alfred Rosenberg.
claims.criticism: While some Nazis were members, the extent of the Thule Society's influence on Nazi ideology is debated. Many other factors contributed to the rise of Nazism.

claims.claim: The Thule Society possessed secret occult knowledge and sought to revive ancient Aryan traditions.
claims.evidence: The society's focus on Germanic mythology, runes, and Atlantis suggests an interest in esoteric knowledge.
claims.criticism: The society's occult practices were largely based on existing esoteric and Theosophical ideas, and their claims of ancient Aryan wisdom are unsubstantiated.

claims.claim: The Thule Society sought to establish a Fourth Reich based on occult principles.
claims.evidence: Some Thule Society members expressed a desire for a new world order based on Aryan supremacy.
claims.criticism: This claim is largely based on speculation and interpretations of the society's esoteric beliefs. There is no concrete evidence of a plan to establish a Fourth Reich based solely on occult principles.

keyFigures.name: Karl Haushofer
keyFigures.role: Influential figure associated with the Thule Society and geopolitics

keyFigures.name: Nicholas Goodrick-Clarke
keyFigures.role: Historian who critically examined the Thule Society and its relationship to Nazism

timeline.event: Thule Society sponsors the Deutsche Arbeiterpartei (DAP)
DAP becomes the National Socialist German Workers' Party (NSDAP), or Nazi Party
